courses.title: Fundamentals of Biology
courses.instructor: Takahashi, Kathy, Morris, Kimo, Jang, Charles J., Oertel, Patricia, Dinh-Mahavongtrakul, Minhan L., Beltran, Jennie L.
courses.description: Principles of biology stressing the relationship of all organisms from anatomical, physiological, and ecological points of view. Includes cell machinery, genetics, reproduction, embryology, animal behavior, botany, ecology, evolution, and human physiology. Concurrent enrollment in Biology 109L recommended. Designed for non-biology majors. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Biotechnology
courses.description: This course is a general examination of biology as it relates to the field of biotechnology. Topics include the fundamental chemical processes common in prokaryotic and eukaryotic biology, chemistry of bio-molecules (proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids and lipids), cellular and molecular biology, basic immunology, and classical and molecular genetics with an emphasis on gene expression and genetic engineering. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Medical Terminology and Documentation for the Occupational Therapy Assistant (OTA)
courses.description: This course will offer an introduction to basic medical terminology and documentation appropriate to practice needs of the occupational therapy assistant. Prerequisites: Biology 149 or Biology 239 and Biology 249 with a minimum grade of C and Must be accepted into the Occupational

courses.title: Calculus for Biological, Management and Social Sciences
courses.instructor: Vu, Dahlia N., Oh, Suanne C., Nguyen, Kelly K., Shahbazian, Roy A., Lai, Karen A., Tolentino, Justin A., Solheid, Christa L.
courses.description: Single and multi-variable calculus including limits, derivatives, integrals, exponentials, and logarithmic functions and partial derivatives. Applications are drawn from biology, social science, and business. Prerequisites: College algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: College algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

courses.title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology
courses.instructor: Robertson, Mario C., Cundiff, Wendy H., Evans, Tracy C.
courses.description: An introduction to humankind's place in nature including evolutionary theory, principles of genetics, primate evolution and behavior, fossil evidence for human evolution, human biology and variation, growth and adaptability, and biomedical anthropology. Includes practical application of biological anthropology to human problems. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Biological Anthropology Laboratory
courses.instructor: Robertson, Mario C., Leroy, Ailissa N.
courses.description: Laboratory exercises and experiments designed to explore and understand the primary areas of physical anthropology: evolutionary theory, principles of genetics, comparative anatomy, physiology, behavior and ecology of vertebrates with an emphasis on nonhuman primates, analysis of fossil evidence for human evolution, human biology and variation, growth and adaptability, and biomedical anthropology. Includes both traditional and virtual laboratory experiences. Prerequisites: Anthropology 101 with a minimum grade of C or concurrent enrollment in Anthropology 101.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Anthropology 101 with a minimum grade of C or concurrent enrollment in Anthropology 101.

courses.title: Nutrition
courses.instructor: Mathot, Sarah H., Grabowski, Alicia C., Rickrode, Taylor M.
courses.description: This course examines the biological basis of human nutrition through the integration of biology, physiology, biochemistry, and microbiology. Using the scientific method, students analyze how nutrients are digested, absorbed, and metabolized, and evaluate the effects of deficiencies and excesses across the lifecycle. Emphasis is placed on hypothesis testing, evidence-based reasoning, and the interdependency of the sciences to explain nutrition's role in health and disease. Topics include dietary standards, nutrient metabolism, food selection, physical activity, biotechnology, and issues of equity in food access. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Fire Protection Organization
courses.instructor: Horner, Stephen D., Busch, Michael E., Conroy, Daniel J., Lunde, Ty R., Flint, James H., Mead, Frank H.
courses.description: This course provides an overview to fire protection and emergency services; career opportunities in fire protection and related fields; culture and history of emergency services; fire loss analysis; organization and function of public and private fire protection services; fire departments as part of local government; laws and regulations affecting the fire service; fire service nomenclature; specific fire protection functions; basic fire chemistry and physics; introduction to fire protection systems; introduction to fire strategy and tactics; life safety initiatives. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Single Variable Calculus II
courses.instructor: Everett, Michael C., Ro, Kelly, Lui, Rachel T., Sweeney, George F., Mishal, Amit A., CongHuyen, Natalie, Lopez, Saul, Le, Huyen N., Not Listed, Torres Toral, Juan J.
courses.description: Applications of integrals, including volumes, work, arc length, and surface area. Integration techniques, differential equations, conics, parametric equations, polar coordinates, improper integrals, sequences, and infinite series. Prerequisites: Mathematics 180 or Mathematics 180H with a minimum grade of C.

Prerequisites: Intermediate algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

courses.title: Introduction to Programming
courses.description: This introduction C++ programming course is project-oriented. Students will learn the C++ programming language and syntax for solving problems, and utilize MS Visual Studio IDE (Integrated Development Environment) to edit, compile, test, debug code and deploy computer programs. The course emphasizes computer science concepts: data types, mathematical operations, I/O (input/output) operations, conditions, iterations, and functions. In addition, software design principles, practices, and utilizing structured methodologies will be covered. Prerequisites: Completion of intermediate algebra or higher or eligibility for transfer-level mathematics based on multiple measures.

courses.instructor: Ro, Kelly, Everett, Michael C., Ghaffari, Ardeshir
courses.description: A first course in differential and integral calculus of a single variable. Topics include limits and continuity of functions, techniques and applications of differentiation, an introduction to integration, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. This course is primarily intended for Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) majors. Limits and continuity, derivatives and integrals of algebraic, trigonometric, and other transcendental functions. Applications including extrema tests, related rates, and areas. courses.title: Personal Finance
courses.description: This course is designed to empower individuals with various tools to manage their money and make sound lifelong financial decisions. Topics include financial planning, income and expense management, investment analysis, retirement planning, consumerism, insurance, estate planning, home ownership and credit management. This course uses financial, tax, and mathematical calculations. Students will take a hands-on approach to such learning and skill development with problem-solving assignments and projects that require them to explain information presented in tax forms and financial statements. They will also obtain the knowledge and skills required to convert financial information into portrayals that contribute to an understanding of present and future personal and family financial needs. Students will also be provided with opportunities to analyze and synthesize on documents and reports. They will develop the logical reasoning skills needed by informed and productive citizens. Moreover, instructors will (a) provide student-centered activity-based instruction, (b) emphasize the conceptual understanding and skills of mathematics and quantitative reasoning, and (c) facilitate the development of competence and confidence in problem-solving abilities. Prerequisites: Intermediate algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Intermediate algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

courses.title: Introduction to Statistics
courses.description: This course is an introduction to statistical thinking and processes, including methods and concepts for discovery and decision-making using data. Topics include descriptive statistics; probability and sampling distributions; statistical inference; correlation and linear regression; analysis of variance, chi-squared, and t-tests; and application of technology for statistical analysis including the interpretation of the relevance of the statistical findings. Students apply methods and processes to applications using data from a broad range of disciplines. This course has embedded support. Formerly: Math 219S - Statistics and Probability with Support. courses.title: Statistics for the Behavioral Sciences
courses.instructor: Youssef, Marine T., Motoyoshi, Rachel L., Castillo, Ricardo R., Yimenu, Bethlehem T., Ortiz, Fernando
courses.description: Introduces psychology and behavioral science majors to descriptive and inferential statistical methods. Knowledge of these methods is essential to the understanding, interpretation, and performance of scientific research. Topics covered include probability theory, hypothesis testing, correlation, analysis of variance, the graphical representation of data, basic research design, and the use of computer software to perform statistical analyses. Prerequisites: Intermediate algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Intermediate algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

courses.title: Google Analytics & SEO Applications Statistics Apps for Business
courses.description: Use Google Analytics and Search Engine Optimization (SEO) tools to measure and improve website performance. Practice keyword research, content optimization, traffic analysis, and campaign tracking with Google Analytics and related web statistics apps. Apply SEO techniques and analytics reports to evaluate conversions, return on investment (ROI), and site effectiveness. Integrate AI tools such as Google Gemini and ChatGPT to generate insights, optimize content, and support data-driven decisions. Complete hands-on projects to apply analytics and SEO skills to real-world business scenarios. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Survey of English Literature II
courses.description: Introductory study of representative selections from the English Romantic Movement to the present. Emphasis on those authors best exemplifying their period, such as Austen, Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, the Shelleys, Keats, Tennyson, Newman, Carlyle, the Brownings, Dickens, the war poets, Houseman, Yeats, Wilde and Woolf. Prerequisites: English 101 or English 101H with a minimum grade of C.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: English 101 or English 101H with a minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Business Interpretation and Translation - Spanish/English
courses.description: Business Spanish and English bilingual skills required for interpreters and translators to provide professional interpreting and translating services to business, government, and education. Topics include proper translation of communications, industry terminology, forms, Individualized Educational Plan (IEP) forms, social media, marketing, advertising, presentations, government documents, three methods of interpretation (Consecutive, Simultaneous, and Sight Translation), professional role, standards, and code of ethics. Topics also include exploring AI tools in interpreting and translating. Students must be fluent in both Spanish and English (oral and written) to be confirmed by instructor with first assignment. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Medical Interpretation and Translation - Spanish / English
courses.description: Medical Spanish and English bilingual skills required for interpreters and translators to provide professional interpreting and translating services to medical offices, hospitals, dental offices, clinics, and other healthcare services. Topics include proper translation of medical terminology, professional role, standards, code of ethics, medical signs and symptoms, health insurance, medical forms, and three methods of interpretation (Consecutive, Simultaneous, and Sight Translation). courses.title: Language and Culture
courses.description: General introduction to the processes of human communication emphasizing coextensive aspects of language and culture. Surveys core areas of linguistic anthropology: structural linguistics; biological basis of language; and sociolinguistics. Topics include acquisition of first and second languages, languages in contract, and the effects of both language and culture on inter/intra group communication. Languages spoken in the local area are used as basis of study. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Law and Literature
courses.description: This course explores the intersection between law and literature, examining how legal concepts and issues are represented and interpreted through literary texts. Students will analyze various works of fiction, drama, and poetry that engage with legal themes such as justice, rights, and morality. The course will also delve into how literature reflects and critiques legal systems and practices and how they impact individuals and communities, as well as how legal frameworks influence literary production and interpretation. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Bachelor of Arts in Paralegal Studies program.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Bachelor of Arts in Paralegal Studies program.

courses.title: Survey of Art From the Renaissance to Contemporary
courses.description: This course provides an overview of art and architecture from the Renaissance to the contemporary period with a focus on art from Europe. Art movements and individual painters, sculptors, architects, and printmakers will be presented within the context of the social, political, and intellectual histories of their respective periods. Required for art majors. Formerly: ART 102 - Survey of Western Art History II: Renaissance to Today. courses.title: Survey of Art From Prehistory to the Medieval Era

courses.title: Health and Social Justice
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the health inequities in the United States that stem from unequal living conditions. Students will explore how education, socioeconomic status, racism and gender shape health epidemics and policy development. The basic skills necessary for advocating for health and social justice will be theoretically demonstrated. Prerequisites: English, one level below transfer (i.e., eligibility for English composition (C-ID ENGL 100) and a reading course

courses.title: Introduction to Vietnamese Bilingual Interpreting
courses.description: Introduction to Vietnamese and English bilingual skills for a career as an interpreter or translator. Learn about the various industries that employ bilingual skills to provide interpreting and translating services for business, medical, legal, government, and education. Course includes proper translation of industry terminology, written and verbal translation techniques, three methods of interpretation (Consecutive, Simultaneous, and Sight Translation), professional role, and code of ethics. Topics also include exploring AI tools in interpreting and translating. Students must be fluent in both Vietnamese and English (oral and written) to be confirmed by instructor with first assignment. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Public Health
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the discipline of Public Health. Students will gain an understanding of the basic concepts and terminologies of public health, and the history and accomplishments of public health officials and agencies. An overview of the functions of various public health professions and institutions, and an in-depth examination of the core public health disciplines is covered. Topics of the discipline include the epidemiology of infectious and chronic disease; prevention and control of diseases in the community including the analysis of the social determinants of health and strategies for eliminating disease, illness and health disparities among various populations; community organizing and health promotion programming; environmental health and safety; global health; and healthcare policy and management. Prerequisites: English, one level below transfer (i.e., eligibility for English composition (C-ID ENGL 100) and a reading course

courses.title: Business Writing
courses.description: Overview of oral and written communication skills used in business. Emphasizes guidelines for improving writing and speaking skills, common solutions to common communication problems, ethical issues facing business communicators today, instructions on how to identify areas of legal vulnerability, and tested techniques for communicating successfully in today's high-tech, international business environment. Prerequisites: English 101 or 101H with a minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Technology for Interpreters & Translator
courses.instructor: Strong, Nayeli P., Vargas, Cesar A.
courses.description: Learn the technology and equipment used by professional interpreters and translators. Develop skills in using hardware and software for in-person and remote interpreting and translating. Topics include emerging technologies such as AI tools, hands-on exercises comparing AI output with human translation, and evaluating the reliability and limitations of digital tools. Course content emphasizes ethical decision-making, professional standards, and practical applications. Integrating modern technology will help increase efficiency, accuracy, and productivity in written translation and verbal interpreting. Bilingual proficiency in English and Spanish or Vietnamese is strongly recommended. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Analyzing and Applying Teacher Strategies in the Classroom
courses.description: This course prepares pre-service early childhood and early elementary education teachers to effectively use formative and summative assessments to understand and support the developmental, academic, and social-emotional progress of young children in Preschool-Third Grade classrooms. Candidates will learn to use assessment data to inform instruction that is developmentally appropriate, intentional, and aligned with content standards. The course emphasizes the design of inclusive learning environments that integrate Universal Design for Learning (UDL) principles and apply a Multi-Tiered System of Support (MTSS) framework to address diverse learner needs. Special attention will be given to culturally and linguistically responsive teaching practices, strategies for supporting English learners, and building strong partnerships with families. Students are required to complete 25 hours of structured observation and fieldwork experience in an approved early childhood and/or elementary school classroom setting. Prerequisites: By the 1st week of the class, verification of the state-mandated Tdap vaccination, MMR immunization, flu

courses.title: Introduction to LGBTQ+ Studies
courses.description: The Introduction to LGBTQ+ Studies offers an intersectional examination of issues related to the gay, lesbian, bisexual, transgender, and queer experience. Taught from the sociological perspective, this course includes historical and contemporary analyses of the LGBTQ+ community and their relationship to social institutions, and institutional power. The Introduction to LGBTQ+ Studies also surveys political, legal, and cultural values and actions with a focus on social justice. Field trips may be required. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Intro to Race and Ethnicity
courses.description: This course focuses on the application of sociological theories and concepts to the study of race, ethnicity, and racism within an intersectional context. It includes an examination of the cultural, political, historical, and economic practices and institutions that construct, perpetuate, or challenge racism, racialized inequalities, and interactive dynamics within and between various racial and ethnic groups. Special attention will be extended to the impacts of globalization, immigration, miscegenation, identity formation, and strategies to combat and decrease prejudice and discrimination. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
courses.instructor: Cundiff, Wendy H., Cheetham, David T., Robertson, Mario C., Evans, Tracy C., Plascencia, Moises M.
courses.description: A cross-cultural survey of the major areas of cultural anthropology including subsistence patterns, economic and political systems, family and kinship, religion, and cultural change. Also includes contemporary issues facing humankind such as the environment, resource depletion, ethnic conflict, globalization, and warfare. Emphasis is on understanding cultural diversity and cultural universals. Field trips may be required. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Language and Culture
courses.description: General introduction to the processes of human communication, emphasizing coextensive aspects of language and culture. Surveys core areas of linguistic anthropology: structural linguistics; biological basis of language; and sociolinguistics. Topics include acquisition of first and second languages, languages in contact, and the effects of both language and culture on inter/intra group communication. Languages spoken in the local area are used as basis of study. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Archaeology
courses.description: This is a survey course in world archaeology. Methods of archaeological survey and excavation will be discussed as well as past and current concepts and theories. courses.title: Planet Earth: Introduction to Earth Science
courses.instructor: Coyne, Claire M., Pomeroy, Diana L., Macias, Anthony A., Not Listed
courses.description: This introductory course surveys the major fields of earth science including geology, astronomy, oceanography and meteorology. The course will provide students with a comprehensive overview of the dynamic and interconnected systems that shape our planet and how changes in any aspect of the atmosphere, oceans, or on land effect all other areas. Not open to students who are enrolled, or have credit in Geology 101 or Geography 101. Optional half-day field trip. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Principles/Micro
courses.instructor: Wang, Wei-Chung, Levine, Michael D., Ames, Travis J., Allen, Mason A.
courses.description: Introduction to microeconomics, including basic economic concepts, analysis of markets, efficiency, consumer and firm behavior, industry structures, market failure, and resource markets. For economics, business, and certain engineering and computer science majors. Prerequisites: Elementary Algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Elementary Algebra or equivalent, or a qualifying profile based on multiple measures.

courses.title: Java Programming
courses.description: This is an introduction Java programming course. Students will learn the Java programming language and syntax for solving problems, and utilize an integrated development environment (IDE) for developing Java applications, and Java virtual machine (JVM) to execute Java programs. The course emphasizes computer science concepts: data types, mathematical operations, I/O (input/output) operations, conditions, iterations, and functions. In addition, software design principles, practices, and utilizing structured methodologies will be covered. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Legal Environment of Business
courses.instructor: Hersey, Jonathan, Michael, Eric M., Harman, James C., Sauber, David T., Not Listed, Robinson, Kristen J.
courses.description: Fundamental legal principles pertaining to business transactions. Introduction to the law as an instrument of social and political control in society. Topics include sources of law and ethics, contracts, torts, agency, judicial and administrative processes, employment law, forms of business organizations, and domestic and international governmental regulations. courses.title: Native Americans in the U.S.
courses.description: A historical and contemporary survey of Native Americans in the United States, examining the diverse and complex cultural, religious, and political systems which governed Indigenous societies before and after European contact and colonization, including the development of tribes and nations, and the cultural practices of Native Americans today. courses.title: Fashion Selection
courses.description: This course will examine the interrelatedness of socio-psychological, economic, and political/religious influences on dress. Cross-cultural analysis and interpretation of Western and non-Western clothing behavior. Apparel selection is based on historical perspective, culture, and fashion trends. The fashion professional uses this information to better design, produce, and select products to meet the needs of a culturally diverse consuming population. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to LGBTQ+ Studies
courses.description: The Introduction to LGBTQ+ Studies offers an intersectional examination of issues related to the gay, lesbian, bisexual, transgender, and queer experience. This course includes historical and contemporary analyses of the LGBTQ+ community and their relationship to social institutions, and institutional power. The Introduction to LGBTQ Studies also surveys political, legal, and cultural values and actions with a focus on social justice. Field trips may be required. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Fundamentals of Business
courses.instructor: Shweiri, Gabriel R., Grant, Madeline A., Kowsari, Ali, Kilroy, Michael R.
courses.description: A survey in business providing a multidisciplinary examination of how culture, society, economic systems, legal, international, political, financial institutions, and human behavior interact to affect a business organization's policy and practices within the U.S. and a global society. Demonstrates how these influences impact the primary areas of business including: organizational structure and design; leadership, human resource management, organized labor practices; marketing; organizational communication; technology; entrepreneurship; legal, accounting, financial practices; the stock and securities market; and therefore affect a business' ability to achieve its organizational goals. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Ethnic Studies
courses.instructor: Camacho, Julian S., Herrera, Raul M., Pennings, Megan, Yang, Kayhlia C., Vargas, Karina, Suarez, Jessica, Carmona Leon, Maricelia, Gonzalez, Teresa C., Rivera, Marisol, Tran, Julie, Sanchez, Cecilia R., Not Listed, Valdovinos, Claribel
courses.description: This course serves to broaden the ethnic and racial perspectives of students interested in American culture and society. A trans-disciplinary, multisectoral, and multidimensional approach will be employed to study the social, cultural, political, and economic aspects of four ethnic groups -- Native/First People Americans, African/Black Americans, Chicana/Chicano/Chican@/Chicanx/Xicana/Xicano/Xica nx/Latin/Latina /Latino/Latine/Latinx Americans, and Asian Americans. Emphasis is placed on these groups' historical and contemporary relationships with each other and the rest of American society-Duplicate credit not granted for ETHN 150H. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Chicana/Chicano Studies
courses.instructor: Gonzalez, Teresa C., Valles, Rod, Valdovinos, Claribel, Carmona Leon, Maricelia, Suarez, Jessica, Vargas, Karina, Camacho, Julian S., Herrera, Raul M., Pennings, Megan
courses.description: This course introduces the field of Chicana/Chicano/Chican@/Chicanx/Xicana/Xicano/Xica nx Studies. It is designed to acquaint students with the most significant social, political, economic, and historical aspects of the Chicana/Chicano/Chican@/Chicanx/Xicana/Xicano/Xica nx experience in the United States. A trans-disciplinary, multisectoral, and multidimensional approach will be employed to study Chicanas/Chicanos/Chican@s/Chicanxs/Xicanas/Xicano s/Xicanxs various experiences and their contributions to American society and culture. This course will critically analyze the societal context in which the Chicana/Chicano/Chican@/Chicanx/Xicana/Xicano/Xica nx have sought to maintain their culture. This course is designed to present a foundation in Chicana/Chicano/Chican@/Chicanx/Xicana/Xicano/Xica nx Studies. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Doing Business in Latin America and Asia
courses.description: This course offers an in-depth exploration of the opportunities and challenges of conducting business in Latin America and Asia. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the cultural, linguistic, legal, and structural differences that influence business practices in these regions. This course explores approaches that companies use to compare unique competitive advantages that exist in the regional markets within Latin America to that of the growing globalization of markets within Asia-Pacific regions. The main themes of this subject cover economics, culture, governance, trade, business, strategy and marketing. Prerequisites:

courses.title: AI Ethics for Business
courses.description: Are you curious or concerned about the ethical issues of using AI? This course will help you with knowledge, tools, and strategies for handling ethical issues and concerns the use of Generative AI increases. Explore the moral and societal questions that come with using AI applications and technology for business, education, government, and personal use. Topics include data privacy, fairness in algorithms, transparency, reliability, and emerging AI regulations. Evaluate AI risks, ethical considerations, and challenges. Develop strategies for using AI responsibly. This course is for new and experienced AI users. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Global Supply Chain and Logistics
courses.description: Learn how to respond to international trade inquiries, understand international trade terms of sale for import and export, and how to calculate landed costs. Learn how to create and issue import and export documents used in selling or buying products globally. Includes the creation and use of actual documents used in global trade. Learn how to ship products internationally and develop a transportation strategy. Learn about transportation terminology, the role of the freight forwarder, transportation documentation, freight cost calculations, major ports of the world, and distribution methods. Create documents for product inspection, health/agricultural registrations, and hazardous materials shipments. Utilize actual documents, software, and resources necessary to effectively complete documentation required for international trade. Get introduced to supply chain management and inventory control. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Innovation and Entrepreneurship
courses.description: Discover how the entrepreneurial mindset teaches life skills that can make you more successful now and at every stage of your life. courses.title: Global Marketing: Selling Around the World
courses.description: This comprehensive Global Marketing course explores the dynamic and evolving landscape of international business, equipping students with the tools and strategies needed to successfully market products and services in global markets. courses.title: Principles of Management
courses.instructor: Shweiri, Gabriel R., Kilroy, Michael R.
courses.description: Principles of Management provides students with an overview of the role of management in business. This course is for students who plan on becoming supervisors and managers in their field. It introduces students to the theories, terminology and foundations of management including the functional areas of planning, organizing, leading, and controlling within the context of social and cultural perspectives related to gender, age, race and ethnicity. courses.title: Cloud GIS with ArcGIS Online
courses.description: Develop skills in using ArcGIS Online (AGOL) to build, manage, and share interactive maps, layers, and data in the cloud. Gain hands-on experience publishing vector data, setting symbology, configuring pop-ups, and designing web apps and dashboards. Emphasis is placed on practical applications of cloud GIS for business, public works, environmental monitoring, and public safety projects. Explore how AI tools are beginning to automate spatial data analysis and pattern recognition. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Contract Management & Corporate Documents
courses.description: Discover the role of a paralegal in contract drafting, lifecycle management, and negotiations. courses.title: Financial Accounting
courses.instructor: Weinraub, Jeremy S., Strong, John H., Castillo, Silvia, McCallick, Mark E., Sung, Linda, Wilson, Stephen B., Erlandson, Stephen C., Not Listed, Hoekstra, Thomas R., Weston, Aubrey L.
courses.description: The study of accounting as an information system, examining why it is important, and how it is used by investors and creditors to make decisions. Coverage includes the accounting information system and the recording and reporting of business transactions with a focus on the accounting cycle, the application of generally accepted accounting principles, the classified financial statements, and statement analysis. It also includes issues relating to asset, liability, and equity valuation, revenue and expense recognition, cash flow, internal controls and ethics. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Microsoft Outlook
courses.description: Learn how business professionals use Microsoft Outlook to manage communication, schedules, and tasks effectively. Apply best practices for email, calendar organization, meeting invites, appointment scheduling, contact management, and email security. Explore customization options and integration with other Microsoft applications to improve workplace productivity. Apply AI tools (e.g., Microsoft Copilot) to streamline inbox management, draft professional emails, and organize projects more efficiently. Includes preparation for the Microsoft Office Specialist (MOS) Outlook exam, earning an industry-recognized certification valued by employers. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Managerial Accounting
courses.instructor: Strong, John H., McCallick, Mark E., Weston, Aubrey L., Erlandson, Stephen C., Bowman, Sean P.
courses.description: Study of the use and reporting of accounting data for managerial planning, cost control, and decision making purposes. The course includes broad coverage of concepts, classifications, and behaviors of costs. Topics include cost systems, the analysis and use of cost information, cost-volume-profit analysis, contribution margin, profit planning, standard costs, relevant costs, and capital budgeting. Prerequisites: Accounting 101 with a minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) I
courses.description: This course will cover Federal and California individual income tax theories and laws to prepare students to successfully pass the IRS-provided online Basic, Advanced, and additional tax exams and be eligible to volunteer in the IRS-sponsored Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) program. The successful completion of the course and passing the exams will prepare students to work as an intern or volunteer at a VITA site to prepare tax returns and have face-to-face interaction with taxpayers. Prerequisites: Accounting 104 with a minimum grade of C.

This course is ideal for students of all ages who want to type quickly and accurately for school, work, or personal use. Supplemental activities may use AI applications (e.g., Microsoft Copilot) to create workplace-style typing practice with emails, reports, and online communication. Typing skills with a minimum 20 words per minute (WPM) using proper techniques or completion of BA 110 are recommended. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Automotive Emerging Technologies
courses.description: This course will cover emerging technologies found on modern automotive vehicles. Topics will include advanced navigation systems, WiFi on vehicles, smart and connected cars, Controller Area Network (CAN) systems, Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), collision avoidance systems, driverless vehicles, and auto-pilot systems. New drivetrain systems will also be included, such as electric vehicle motors and high voltage systems, along with advancements in engine tuning and computer programming. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Ethical Hacking
courses.description: This course provides an in-depth understanding of how to protect and defend IT infrastructure. The course combines ethical hacking methodologies with the hands-on application of security tools to secure computer and other digital systems. Students are introduced to common countermeasures that effectively reduce and/or mitigate attacks. In addition, the course covers what an ethical hacker is and how important it is to protect data from cyber attacks. Students will review TCP/IP concepts and practice footprinting, scanning, enumeration, exploitation, and social engineering. Students will be prepared to take CompTIA PenTest+ and/or Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) certification. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Academic Research and Information Literacy
courses.description: This course offers an in-depth exploration of information literacy as an essential academic, civic, and professional skill. Students will critically engage with complex information environments to develop advanced research strategies, evaluate diverse sources across disciplines, and synthesize information ethically and effectively. Emphasis is placed on scholarly communication, data and media literacy, algorithmic influence on information access, and the social and systemic forces shaping knowledge production. This course prepares students for upper-division coursework, transfer-level research, and lifelong learning in an evolving information landscape. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to an approved baccalaureate program at a California Community College.

courses.title: AI Literacy
courses.description: This course introduces students to the foundational concepts, ethical considerations, and practical applications of artificial intelligence (AI) in everyday life, education, and the workplace. INFO 105 emphasizes critical thinking, digital literacy, and responsible information use in AI-powered environments. Students will explore topics such as generative AI tools, algorithmic bias, data privacy, and the impact of AI on research, creativity, and decision-making, as well as the ethical concerns and ramifications of the field. Through hands-on activities and discussions, students will develop the skills to evaluate, interact with, and use AI technologies responsibly and effectively. Prerequisites:

Course discusses quality theory as well advanced quality operations such as, inspection planning, calibration systems, sampling, quality tools including SPC and their implementation, auditing, corrective and preventative action, lean manufacturing concepts, and customer and supplier relationships. Prerequisites: Manufacturing Technology 115 with a minimum grade of C Manufacturing Technology 114 with a minimum grade of C

courses.title: Arrangement and Description
courses.description: Basic skills for arranging and describing archival collections and establishing physical and intellectual control, such as accession records, common arrangement systems, and determining processing priorities. Experience with archival management systems, such as ArchivesSpace, the key components of finding aids, and descriptive standards (DACS and EAD) utilized for effective organization and access to archival materials. Prerequisites: Library Technology 104 and Library Technology 108 with a minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Building Construction for Fire Protection
courses.instructor: Busch, Michael E., Conroy, Daniel J., Muir, Jon C.
courses.description: This course provides the components of building construction related to firefighter and life safety. Prerequisites: Fire Technology 101 and 102 with minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Fire Prevention
courses.instructor: Freeman, Suzanne, Muir, Jon C., Van Mullem, Janice M.
courses.description: This course provides fundamental knowledge relating to the field of fire prevention. Topics include: history and philosophy of fire prevention; community risk reduction; organization and operation of a fire prevention bureau; use and application of codes and standards; plan review; fire inspections; fire and life safety education; and fire investigation. Prerequisites: Fire Technology 101 and 102 with minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Advanced Legal Ethics
courses.description: This course explores the ethical duties of lawyers and paralegals in client representation, focusing on the regulatory role of the State Bar of California. Topics include the supervision of nonlawyer staff, law office procedures for client funds, IOLTA programs, the impact of technology on confidentiality, and ethical considerations in ADR, non-profit, government, and criminal law practices. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

courses.title: Advanced Legal Research, Analysis, and Writing
courses.description: In this advanced legal research and writing course, students will explore sophisticated research techniques and strategies utilizing industry-standard electronic databases. Students will engage in substantive legal analysis, applying current law to various factual scenarios through comprehensive writing exercises. The course will emphasize effective strategies for formulating legal research queries, enabling students to conduct thorough and efficient legal research using electronic resources, databases, and technology. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

courses.title: Medical Insurance Billing and Coding
courses.instructor: Valencia, Briseyda, Luke, Becki J., Seitz, Carol A.
courses.description: Medical Insurance Billing and Coding includes guidelines, regulations, completion of insurance claims, and program information for Medicare, Medicaid/MediCal, TRICARE, Medi/Medi, State Disability, Workers Compensation, and private commercial insurance. courses.title: Drones & The Law
courses.description: Explore the rules and real-world situations that affect drone pilots in this informative short course. Taught by a drone pilot police officer or a drone attorney, the course explains how federal, state, and local laws apply to recreational, commercial, and public safety drone users. Topics include FAA regulations, FAA TRUST Recreational and FAA Part 107 certifications, privacy and liability issues, drone insurance, and examples from actual drone-related legal cases. Discover how new technology, including AI-assisted drones, is raising new questions about privacy and responsibility. This is an informational course with no drone flying activities. Prerequisites:

courses.title: American Sign Language I
courses.instructor: Smith, Elizabeth A., Arazi, Heather L., Ruiz, Karen A., McCrory, Roy K., Nielsen, Marie E., Vollmar, Jazmin M., Arazi, Dana J., Gamache, Diane C.
courses.description: This entry level course is designed to introduce students to American Sign Language (ASL) and fingerspelling as it is used within American Deaf culture. Instruction includes preparation for visual/gestural communication followed by intensive work on comprehension through receptive language skills, development of basic conversational skills, modeling of grammatical structures, and general information about American Deaf culture. ASL 110 is equivalent to two years of high school ASL. Students are required to attend at least one off-campus event. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Digital Illustration I
courses.description: Explores the essentials of illustration for graphic designers and concept artists, using industry-standard production and design tools. Focuses on professional studio techniques to master this essential design tool for the creation of sophisticated graphics. Emphasizes efficient workflow, technical proficiency, and aesthetic judgment. Students produce portfolio-ready projects using a broad range of imaging skills. The primary software used includes Adobe Photoshop, supplemented with Adobe Illustrator, and some time-based media software. Prerequisites: Digital Media 195 or Art 195 or portfolio exhibiting equivalent skills to 3 units of entry-level digital design

courses.title: Digital Illustration II: Advanced Topics
courses.description: Examines advanced design and illustration techniques using industry-standard software via Adobe Creative Cloud. Addresses contemporary illustrative problems, concepts, and skills development within the digital media and graphic arts industries for the creation of unique illustrations. The technical needs for graphic illustrations for print, animation, film, and the web will also be addressed. Primary Software: Adobe Illustrator, supplemented with Adobe Photoshop. Prerequisites: Digital Media 162 or Art 162, or equivalent, as demonstrated in illustration portfolio.

courses.title: Digital Illustration I
courses.description: Explores the essentials of illustration for graphic designers and concept artists, using industry-standard production and design tools. Focuses on professional studio techniques to master this essential design tool for the creation of sophisticated graphics. Emphasizes efficient workflow, technical proficiency, and aesthetic judgment. Students produce portfolio-ready projects using a broad range of imaging skills. The primary software used includes Adobe Photoshop, supplemented with Adobe Illustrator, and some time-based media software. Prerequisites: Digital Media 195 or Art 195 is recommended.

courses.title: Introduction to Interpersonal Communication
courses.instructor: Labreau, Katie M., Saterfield, Kalonji L., Cummings, Reyna V., Rehm, David M.
courses.description: Introduction to communication theory, rhetoric, listening, perception, language usage, nonverbal communication, and conflict management. Students participate in oral and written exercises and simulations; these activities are designed to enhance communicative awareness and skills in interpersonal contexts. It is designed to assist students in improving their own interpersonal and oral communication skills. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Group Dynamics
courses.description: Focuses on effective small group presenting and communication. Explores the foundational knowledge and practice of public speaking for successfully communicating informative and persuasive messages within the small group context. Introduces students to the theories, techniques, and methods of small group communication. Develops speaking, participation, and leadership skills of effective communicators within the small group setting. Provides applied practice through faculty supervised and evaluated oral group presentations delivered to a live audience, through group activities, and through project group work. Involves weekly outside of class team meetings. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Human Relations and Organizational Behavior
courses.instructor: Shweiri, Gabriel R., Kilroy, Michael R.
courses.description: This course explores managing human behavior in organizations and is designed to give students exposure to essential theories, practices and methods used within organizations to promote effective behavior. Topics tie human behavior to organizational dynamics, communication, leadership, power structures, and organizational design. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Principles of Graphic Design
courses.description: Introduction to 2D and 3D graphic design principles in visual communication with an introduction to design process, methodology/theory, and problem-solving. Content includes color theory, typography, identity design, and visual perception. This graphic design concept-based course will explore divergent techniques, tools, and practices resulting in the production of effective visual communications reflecting the needs of the expanding media landscape. A perfect pairing with the course DM 113: Introduction to Digital Media Design Principles. Prerequisites: Digital Media 195 is recommended.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Digital Media 195 is recommended.

courses.title: Argumentation and Debate
courses.instructor: Lockwood, Lance R., Durango, Damian A.
courses.description: This course provides students with a foundational understanding of argumentation, debate concepts, and theories essential for constructing, analyzing, and evaluating arguments across various contexts and public speaking settings. Students will explore key principles such as Aristotle's proofs of ethos, pathos, and logos, as well as audience analysis, ethics in argumentation, and critical listening. Additionally, the course emphasizes effective verbal and nonverbal communication strategies for practical argumentation. Students will also learn to assess the strength of evidence and apply logical reasoning to develop and present compelling arguments before live audiences. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Web Design
courses.description: This course examines global issues related to internet culture, web ethics, equity, social justice, in addition to rights and trends related to the web. In the context of cultural and social needs, students will be introduced to web design using digital media tools; graphics, illustrations, text, sound, motion, using software programs for the web. Students will also review how web design has been used to impact commerce advancement, global communication, advertising, entertainment, and self-promotion relate to global and local community needs. Students will be able to apply their knowledge in site development and responsive design. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Psychology of Human Sexuality
courses.description: An overview of human sexuality through a psychological lens with additional focus on biological, sociocultural, and historical perspectives and influences. Topics to be explored will include, but will not be limited to, the following: sexual anatomy and physiological arousal; sexual attitudes and values; sexual behaviors, dysfunction, disorders and treatments; relationships and communication; interconnections to cultural identities (e.g., race, gender identity, sexual orientation, age, religion, etc.); sexual violence; contraception and pregnancy; and sexually transmitted infections and prevention. Current sex norms and various aspects of interpersonal and individual sexual adjustment will also be discussed. (No credit if a student has taken IDS 155). Prerequisites:

courses.title: Child Guidance and Classroom Management
courses.description: This course will define and explore developmentally appropriate expectations about young children's behavior. Students will learn the importance of the educator's knowledge about discipline strategies, effective communication skills, high-quality teacher and child interactions and the development of a skill set to address behavior issues and implement discipline strategies which are in alignment with the undesirable classroom behaviors. Behavior expectations will be defined and research-based behavioral observation skills, strategies, and assessments will be explored that best support children's various behaviors. Each student will create a community resource file of service agencies to include accessible behavior management resource materials that support teachers and students challenged with behavioral issues in the classroom. It is advised that students take this course in conjunction with working in a classroom setting. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Social Work and Human Services
courses.description: The history and philosophy of social work & human services including theoretical frameworks, the function and orientation of social service organizations, and the roles and qualifications of social work and human service workers. Through a multidisciplinary approach, an examination of the impact of cultural, societal, and ethical issues facing the social work and human services field will frame how target populations are served. Through a social justice lens, special attention is given to the evolution of current service delivery systems, their policies and procedures, and the tasks of culturally responsive social workers within those settings. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Cultural Geography
courses.description: An introductory survey of the geography of culture, and the influences of the physical environment on culture, along with the impact of human activity on the environment, and the role of culture within societies and social groups. courses.instructor: Conley, John M., Scott, Jason W.

Prerequisites: Concurrent enrollment in Geography 101.

courses.title: Dynamic Earth: Introduction to Geology
courses.description: This introductory course that covers Earth materials and the processes that develop and modify the composition, structure, and features of the earth. Included is an overview of minerals, rocks, plate tectonics, volcanoes, earthquakes, geologic time, water resources, glaciation, desertification, structural geology, and energy and mineral resources. Students requiring lab experience should enroll in GEOL101L concurrently with GEOL101. Optional field trip offered. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Men's Health Issues
courses.description: Examines the societal, economic, cultural, and gender influences that shape men's health beliefs and practices. Explores specific health issues unique to men such as accessing health care, healthy relationships, domestic abuse, prostate cancer, and alcohol use. Critically examines literature and media to identify interventions within a masculinity framework to improve men's health outcomes. courses.title: Stress Management
courses.instructor: Baquero, Jaymie M., Nyssen, Adam M.
courses.description: This course examines productive and nonproductive stress and the influence of various types of stress on health and wellness. Topics include the physiological aspects of stress and its effects on health, assessments of personal coping style, strategies for coping effectively with stress, relaxation techniques, mindful awareness, and positive self-talk. courses.instructor: Nguyen, Kathy, Finnell, Maram J., Goodwin, Matthew B.
courses.description: Artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the way we learn, work, and think. The rise of AI in education provides innovative opportunities. AI also presents some challenges, rumors, confusion, ethical considerations, and uncertainty. Learn how to embrace AI to enhance education. courses.title: Administrative Law and Regulatory Compliance
courses.description: Examination of administrative law and regulation, focusing on procedure at both the state and federal levels. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the Administrative Procedure Act governing various agencies, including Security and Exchange Commission, Veterans Affairs, the Social Security Administration (SSA), Workers Compensation, Immigration, Public Utilities, Education, and more. Through the exploration of administrative regulation, creation, and compliance in diverse industries, students will develop the expertise needed to navigate and excel in administrative law practice. Emphasis on the unique role of Paralegals in administrative proceedings. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

sections.notes: Students must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies program. Must be completed prior to taking this course. Virtual Hybrid Instruction. A combination of some live streaming instruction via Zoom during the days and times indicated and online instruction. Students log in to Canvas. https://canvas.sac.edu. There will be no instruction on-campus.*** Additional Section Dates *** Last Date to Add (add authorization required) : August 30, 2026. Last Drop Date with W grade: November 8, 2026. Last Drop Date without W grade: August 30, 2026. Last Date to get Refund: August 30, 2026

courses.title: Child Growth and Development
courses.instructor: Not Listed, Navarro, Maria E., Kusumo, Joy Ann S., Lamourelle, Chantal E., Kimmel, Michelle K., Naman, Trudy J.
courses.description: Students examine the progression of development in the physical, cognitive, social, and emotional domains and identify developmental milestones for children from conception through adolescence. Emphasis is on interactions between biological processes, environmental, and cultural factors. Students may engage in various methods of observing children's development to evaluate individual differences and analyze development characteristics at various stages according to developmental theories. A student will not receive credit for this course if they have already taken Psychology 157. Formerly: CDEV 107 - Child Growth and Development (DS1) Prerequisites:

courses.title: Child Growth and Development (DS1)
courses.instructor: Naman, Trudy J., Kimmel, Michelle K., Lamourelle, Chantal E., Navarro, Maria E., King, Tawnie S., Becerra, Christopher G.
courses.description: This course examines the major physical, cognitive, social and emotional developmental milestones for children, from conception through adolescence. Emphasis on interactions between maturational processes and environmental factors. Students will observe children, evaluate individual differences, and analyze characteristics of development at various stages according to developmental theories. (No credit if a student has taken Psychology 157.) Prerequisites:

courses.title: Understanding Child Abuse and Maltreatment
courses.description: Exploration of child maltreatment; this course is designed to address the correlation and potential effects from various forms of child maltreatment. Students will learn some of the possible identifying indicators of a child being maltreatment or potential factors that may be putting the child at risk. Factors such as the historical treatment of children, societal perspectives on children, family responsibilities, family dynamics and generational family dysfunction. Additionally, this course is intended to help students gain better understanding of child maltreatment and trauma in order to best support children. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Infant/Toddler Growth and Development (DS4)
courses.description: A study of infants and toddlers, from conception to 36 months, including the theoretical frameworks of development, and the impact of pregnancy, birth, and early life experiences on a child emotionally, socially, cognitively, and physically. Emphasizes the role of family, culture, caring and responsive adults, and attachment relationships on growth and development. Prerequisites: Child Development - Apprenticeship 107 or Child Development 107 and Child Development - Apprenticeship

courses.title: Brain Development and Learning
courses.description: This class explores the development of the brain for children from birth through adolescence, and how behavior and learning are affected. Brain-based learning strategies will be used to teach new ways of approaching learning, including how to understand diverse learning styles. This course is designed for educators, parents, and students who are interested in knowing more about how the brain operates and how the environment affects the brain. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Lifelong Understanding and Self Development
courses.instructor: Robledo, Reymundo, Capellino Lopez, Yesenia, Macafee, Lisa R., Pineda, Maribel, Perez Martinez, Moises, Muir, Shannon L., Leos, Nigel M., Robledo, Joanna C., Brown, Angela T., Parayno, Jeanell A., Lopez Ediss, Christine, Huynh, Jordan D., Zavala, Janet
courses.description: Integrates concepts of lifelong understanding pertaining to career choice, educational planning, and self inventory. Skills, values, and interest assessments are utilized. Emphasis is on applying psychological principles to values clarification, goal setting, and decision making. Students analyze social/cultural conditioning and explore successful strategies for living in a diverse society. Prerequisites:

courses.title: School-Age Child Care and Recreation Activities
courses.description: This course will focus on school-age creative activities, including planning and implementing an appropriate before/after school curriculum. Attention will be paid to integrating academics, recreation, and creative activities suitable for school-age child care programs. Not offered every semester. Prerequisites: Must be a state-indentured apprentice.

courses.title: Introduction to Lifespan Psychology
courses.description: Introduction to human development from a psychological perspective. Explores human development from conception through death, including biological and environmental influences. Theories and research of physical, cognitive, personality, and social development are examined from classical and contemporary perspectives. Attention will also be given to both normative and non-normative development. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Children With Disabilities Or Delays
courses.instructor: Kimmel, Michelle K., Ashline, Joanna A., Salinas, Phirin K.
courses.description: Introduces the variations in development of children and adolescents with disabilities or delay. Includes an overview of historical and societal influences, laws relating to individuals with disabilities or delays, and the assessment and referral process. Emphasizes the importance of working in partnership with families and specialists to support children's development. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Intermediate Flat Pattern Making
courses.description: A continuation of FDM 109 Flat Pattern Techniques, this course covers intermediate pattern drafting techniques not covered in FDM 109. Students learn to draft patterns for garments using 3D software and also export their patterns to paper to be cut and sewn into fabric samples. Includes development of patterns for various pants (culotte, trouser, slack, dungarees/overalls) and a tailored jacket. Other drafting techniques include various plackets, bias cut dresses, inserted and accordion pockets, various sleeve shapes, kimonos, dolmens, drop shoulders. POM (Point of Measurement) specifications are discussed in relation to both pattern development and finished garments. Prerequisites: Fashion Design and Merchandising 109 with a minimum grade of C.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Fashion Design and Merchandising 109 with a minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Teaching in a Diverse Society
courses.instructor: Nunez, Yvette, Seneviratne, Anushi I., Lopez, Romana, Lamourelle, Chantal E.
courses.description: Examines the historical and current perspectives on diversity and inclusion and the impact of systemic societal influences on children's development, learning, and school experiences. Strategies for developmentally, culturally, and linguistically appropriate anti-bias curriculum will be explored as well as approaches to promote inclusive and anti-racist classroom communities. Includes self-reflection on the influence of teachers' own culture and life experiences on teaching and interactions with children and families. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Criminal Justice
courses.instructor: Gonis Jr, Andrew E., Huskey, Jason K.
courses.description: This course introduces students to the characteristics of the criminal justice system in the United States. Focus is placed on examining crime measurement, theoretical explanations of crime, responses to crime, components of the system, and current challenges to the system. The course examines the evolution of the principles and approaches utilized by the justice system and the evolving forces that have shaped those principles and approaches. Although justice structure and process are examined in a cross cultural context, emphasis is placed on the US justice system, particularly the structure and function of US police, courts, and corrections. Students are introduced to the origins and development of criminal law, legal process, and sentencing and incarceration policies. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Concepts of Criminal Law
courses.description: This course offers an analysis of the doctrines of criminal liability in the United States and the classification of crimes against persons, property, morals, and public welfare. Special emphasis is placed on the classification of crime, the general elements of crime, the definitions of common and statutory law, and the nature of acceptable evidence. This course utilizes case law and case studies to introduce students to criminal law. The completion of this course offers a foundation upon which upper-division criminal justice course will build. The course will also include some limited discussion of prosecution and defense decision making, criminal culpability, and defenses to crimes. Prerequisites:

courses.description: An introductory course using microeconomic models to understand individual decisions by consumers and firms, market outcomes including market failure, elasticity, market structures, labor markets, inequality, and the impact of government policies. Formerly: ECON 121 - Principles/Micro. Prerequisites: Placement as determined by the college's multiple measures assessment process or completion of a course taught at or

courses.title: Psychology of Adjustment
courses.description: Introduction to psychoanalytic, behavioristic, cognitive, humanistic and existential theories of personality as they relate to dealing effectively with the changing demands of everyday life. Covers personal growth, self-concept, stress and coping, personal goals and motivation, and interpersonal relationships in relation to culture, gender, ethnicity, historical cohort, and socio-economic status. Includes exercises for increasing self-awareness, identifying personals goals, and for building skills for effective living and well-being. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Community Interaction
courses.description: Examines the complex, dynamic relationship between communities and the justice system in addressing crime and conflict, with an emphasis on the challenges and prospects of administering justice within a diverse multicultural population. Topics may include the consensus and conflicting values in culture, religion, and law. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Corrections
courses.description: This course provides a history of and critical analysis of punishment, the various types of punishment, alternatives to punishment, and the impact of punishment on the criminal justice system, corrections, a critical examination of the types of correctional institutions and the clients housed in each institution, and an examination of contemporary correctional issues. courses.title: Law and Society
courses.instructor: Robinson, Kristen J., Millett, Joi Q., Smith, Kimberly D., Sauber, David T., Harman, James C.
courses.description: Overview of the legal system including the courts, participants and various sources of law in the American legal system and how society is affected. Review of the legal and judicial process in the United States covering the adversarial system, jurisdiction of federal and state courts, and the general process of judicial review and how different segments of society are affected. Explanation of different sources of law including statutes, court cases and administrative agency rules. Review of basic legal reasoning and introductory legal research methods. Introduction to substantive areas of law and the effect the law has in various groups and individuals in our system. Review of the role and regulation of legal professionals, legal ethics and consumer protection laws, and their effect on providing legal services to various segments of society. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Research Methods in Psychology
courses.instructor: Nardi, Nicholas M., Motoyoshi, Rachel L., Yimenu, Bethlehem T., Castillo, Ricardo R., Youssef, Marine T.
courses.description: This course surveys various psychological research methods with an emphasis on research design, experimental procedures, descriptive methods, instrumentation, and the collection, analysis, interpretation, and reporting of research data. Research design and methodology will be examined through a review of research in a variety of the sub-disciplines of psychology. In laboratory sessions students will conduct experimental and non-experimental research in a variety of areas of psychology. Actual data collected from research conducted during laboratory sessions will be analyzed with statistical software. Prerequisites: Psychology C1000 with a minimum grade of C. Completion of or concurrent enrollment in Psychology 210 or

courses.title: Tort Law and Alternative Dispute Resolution
courses.description: Learn about the different types of Torts including: intentional torts, negligence, strict liability, product liability, damages, immunity, and defenses to torts. Learn how to apply principles of insurance law and procedures for the investigation of personal injury cases. Students will review the principles of dispute resolution through negotiation, mediation, and arbitration. Students will mediate impartially and use tools to maximize negotiation effectiveness. The course includes application of mediation skills in the law office. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Introduction to Psychopathology
courses.description: Introduction to the commonly diagnosed psychological disorders. Includes psychophysiological disorders, anxiety, depression, substance abuse, sexual dysfunctions, schizophrenia, developmental, cognitive, and personality disorders. Emphasis is on identification, symptomatology, etiology, and methods of therapeutic intervention. Prerequisites: Psychology C1000/1000H with a minimum grade of C.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Psychology C1000/1000H with a minimum grade of C.

courses.title: Human Disease and Occupation
courses.description: This class will explore diseases that are commonly seen in occupational therapy practice and the effect they have on participation in occupation. Each disease will be covered in terms of etiology, prognosis, prevention, pathophysiology, medical management, precautions, and lifestyle redesign required as a result of the disease. Prerequisites: Occupational Therapy Assistant 100, 101, 101L, and 110 with a minimum grade of C.

courses.description: This course will focus on leadership and the types of leadership that relate to the work of the OTA. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Occupational Studies program.

courses.title: Advanced Law Office Procedure, Transactions, and Forms
courses.description: Overview of advanced law office procedures, to include: client intake procedures, appropriate selection and use of judicial council forms (including family law, probate, and unlawful detainer), subpoenas and warrants, consumer notices, HIPAA, settlement and demand letters as well as complex calendar rules. Students will develop a comprehensive understanding of these advanced procedures and their application within a law office. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

courses.title: 3D Design - Denim
courses.description: This course will teach students how to create a 3D denim bottom garment in Clo3D/Marvelous Designer. courses.title: The Professional Paralegal
courses.instructor: Millett, Joi Q., Smith, Kimberly D.
courses.description: This is the capstone class to the Paralegal associate degree and certificate. Students will demonstrate their knowledge of ethics, legal principles, and the technical skills necessary for entry level employment as a paralegal. This course is to be taken in the semester of or prior to graduation. This course will include preparation of graduation petitions, professional resumes and a professional portfolio that will include work prepared in PARA 101, PARA 121, PARA 120, PARA 145 and PARA 150. Students will be required to certify they are in the second to last or last semester prior to graduation. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Honors The Borderlands: Cultural Context and Intercultural Relations
courses.description: This course analyzes the United States-Mexican border and its regions. This course dissects the geographical, historical, and artistic significance. Dialogues occur in the context of "borders" and their intersectionality of international, regional, community, intersectional, transnational, and personal contexts concerning interethnic/intercultural relations. Attention is given to the intersectionality of Native American/First People, African/Black American, Chicana/Chicano/Chicanx/Chican@/Xicana/Xicano/Xica nx, Latin American/Latina American/Latino American/Latinx American, and Asian American ethnic groups within mainstream U.S. society. Duplicate credit not granted for ETHN 102. Prerequisites:

courses.title: Advanced Civil Litigation
courses.description: This comprehensive class will cover civil procedure before, during and after trial, civil discovery, attorney fee awards, damages, and appellate practice. Additional topics to be covered will include summary judgments, depositions, expert witnesses, discovery motions, service of process, subpoenas, statutory deadlines, and writs of attachment. Upon completion, paralegal students will have the skills to work under an attorney's supervision to support a thriving litigation practice in California. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

courses.title: Psychology and Law Documents
courses.description: Introduction to the field of psychology and law. This course is meant to provide students with a better understanding of how psychological theory and research apply to the legal system, as well as how the law guides psychological research. It will cover psychological theory and methods relevant to legal topics such as eyewitness memory, expert and jury decision making, race, mental illness, cognitive biases, and social influence. These topics span multiple sub-fields, including cognitive, social, developmental, personality, and biological psychology. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Bachelor's of Arts program in Paralegal Studies.

courses.title: Advanced Criminal Procedure
courses.description: This advanced course will allow students to understand the day-to-day practice realities in a criminal law office through the variety of responsibilities a paralegal would undertake in a busy criminal defense or prosecutors' office. Students will demonstrate their understanding of procedures during a criminal prosecution including arrest & booking; charging instruments; arraignments; bail/bond; pre-trial services reports and supervision; discovery; pretrial motions and hearings; plea agreements; trial; sentencing guidelines, post-sentence supervised release, probation, and parole. Students will evaluate in-custody and out-of-custody filings as well as appraising appropriate court communications and mandatory timelines. Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisites: Student must be admitted to the Paralegal Studies Baccalaureate program.
